an4.175:1.1: Then the Venerable Upavāṇa approached the Venerable Sāriputta … and said to him:
an4.175:2.2: (1) "Friend Sāriputta, does one become an end-maker by means of knowledge?"
an4.175:2.4: (2) "Then does one become an end-maker by means of conduct?"
an4.175:2.6: (3) "Then does one become an end-maker by means of knowledge and conduct?"
an4.175:2.8: (4) "Then does one become an end-maker otherwise than by means of knowledge and conduct?"
an4.175:3.2:
"When you are asked: ‘Friend Sāriputta, does one become an end-maker by means of knowledge?’ you say: ‘This is not the case, friend.’ When you are asked: ‘Then does one become an end-maker by means of conduct? … by means of knowledge and conduct? … otherwise than by means of knowledge and conduct?’
you say: ‘This is not the case, friend.’ Then in what way does one become an end-maker?"
an4.175:4.2: (1) "If, friend, one were to become an end-maker by means of knowledge, even one who still has clinging would become an end-maker.
an4.175:4.3: (2) If one were to become an end-maker by means of conduct, even one who still has clinging would become an end-maker.
an4.175:4.4: (3) If one were to become an end-maker by means of knowledge and conduct, even one who still has clinging would become an end-maker.
an4.175:4.5: (4) If one were to become an end-maker otherwise than by means of knowledge and conduct, then a worldling would be an end-maker; for a worldling is destitute of knowledge and conduct.
an4.175:5.2:
"Friend, one deficient in conduct does not know and see things as they are. One accomplished in conduct
knows and sees things as they are. Knowing and seeing things as they are, one becomes an end-maker."
